messages.body:
If I were thinking about building a portfolio for 2030, there are a few businesses I’d want to really understand first.  
 
$SOFI is shaping the future of financial platforms, while $AMD is at the heart of AI compute powering the next generation of technology. 
 
Then there’s $AMZN, dominating cloud infrastructure and ads.  
 
And $GOOGL isn’t just search-it’s AI, autonomous driving, and so much more.  
 
These names aren’t just stocks, they’re trends. 
 
Which of these do you think will lead the next decade, and which might get disrupted?

messages.body:
Roosters crow. 
 
Birds fly. 
 
Gold shines. 
 
$AMD goes up. 
 
Every week someone explains why it should be lower. Every month the chart disagrees. 
 
At this point I'm starting to think AMD doesn't need good news. It just needs bears to keep writing essays about why the rally can't continue.

messages.body:
Before the semiconductor boom, we were on $NVDA, $AMD, and $ARM early. Everyone talks about the stock, but we were focused on the ETFs behind them. 
 
Watching $SMH and $DRAM act healthy gave us the confidence to buy every pullback. That’s the difference between chasing hype and spotting trends. 
 
Do you guys watch ETFs for clues too, or just rely on news hype? Curious what your approach is.

messages.body:
$AMD bulls may be focused on the wrong part of the story.The real opportunity isn't selling GPUs. It's lowering the cost of inference. 
 
As AI adoption scales, token economics become increasingly important. Training gets the headlines, but inference is where the recurring demand lives. If AMD can deliver meaningful cost advantages at scale, customers will eventually start paying attention.The market has spent years assuming performance alone determines the winner. The next phase of this race may come down to efficiency, operating costs, and total economics. 
That's where things get interesting.

message.body:
I finally exited my $AMD position after a 5x run and moved the proceeds into $NVDA.  
 
Not gonna lie… this one was actually emotional for me.  
 
$AMD has been one of my biggest winners over the years, so selling it almost felt weird after riding the whole move. 
 
But at some point I had to stop looking at the chart emotionally and just focus on the numbers.  
 
By 2028, estimates are roughly: 
 
$AMD → ~43% annual growth 
$NVDA → ~45% annual growth 
 
Yet somehow: 
 
$AMD trades around 30x 2028 earnings 
while $NVDA is closer to 15x  
 
I honestly can’t fully justify that gap anymore. 
 
If AI capex slows, both probably get hit. 
 
But weirdly enough… $NVDA might actually have LOWER downside here simply because the valuation looks much cheaper relative to growth.  
 
So in my head, either: 
1️⃣ $AMD is getting way too expensive 
or 
2️⃣ $NVDA is still undervalued 
 
Personally… I’m betting on the second one.
